messages.content: shorten：Liver fibrosis is a chronic and progressive disease characterized by the excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix components, presenting a significant challenge in the field of medicine. Despite extensive research efforts, effective therapeutic strategies targeting liver fibrosis remain limited. Thus, it is essential to explore innovative approaches for the management of this condition.

Liver fibrosis is a chronic and progressive disease with excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix, posing a major challenge in medicine. Despite extensive research, effective therapies for liver fibrosis are limited. Therefore, exploring innovative approaches is crucial for its management.

shorten： C57BL/6J male mice were used to model liver fibrosis, induced by Carbon tetrachloride (CCl4). The fibrotic mice were treated with interleukin-10 (IL-10) gene and cleared NK cells with an Asialo GM1 (ASGM1) antibody. The mice were divided into five groups: normal controls (NORMAL group), liver fibrosis group (FIBROSIS group), IL-10 gene treatment group (IL-10 group), NK cells clearance group (FIBROSIS/ASGM1 group) and IL-10 gene treatment combined with NK cell clearance group (IL-10/ASGM1 group).

C57BL/6J male mice were used to model liver fibrosis induced by CCl4. Fibrotic mice were treated with IL-10 gene and cleared NK cells using ASGM1 antibody. The mice were categorized into five groups: NORMAL, FIBROSIS, IL-10, FIBROSIS/ASGM1, and IL-10/ASGM1.
shorten：C57BL/6J mice were used to model CCl4-induced liver fibrosis. They were divided into five groups: NORMAL, FIBROSIS, IL-10, FIBROSIS/ASGM1, and IL-10/ASGM1.
C57BL/6J mice were used to model CCl4-induced liver fibrosis, divided into five groups: NORMAL, FIBROSIS, IL-10, FIBROSIS/ASGM1, and IL-10/ASGM1.
shorten：To observe the effect of the presence or absence of NK cells on the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10，fibrotic mice were treated with IL-10 gene and cleared NK cells using ASGM1 antibody.
To study the impact of NK cells on IL-10's anti-fibrotic effect, fibrotic mice were treated with IL-10 gene and NK cells were cleared using ASGM1 antibody.

improve：In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene significantly alleviates liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice.
At the same time, the IL-10 gene promotes the accumulation of NK cells in the liver, promoting the NKG2D and IFN of NK cells- γ Upregulation of CD107a expression and promotion of NK cell development. In addition, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 is significantly inhibited.

In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene effectively reduces liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice. Additionally, the IL-10 gene enhances the accumulation of NK cells in the liver, leading to increased expression of NKG2D, IFN-γ, and CD107a, and promoting NK cell development. Moreover, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the liver's CCL5 level. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 is significantly hindered.

In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene effectively alleviates liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice. It promotes the accumulation of NK cells in the liver, leading to increased expression of NKG2D, IFN-γ, and CD107a, thereby enhancing NK cell development. The IL-10 gene also regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the liver's CCL5 level. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 is significantly hindered.

improve：In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene significantly alleviates liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice.
At the same time, the IL-10 gene promotes the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and promotes NKG2D and IFN of NK cells- γ Upregulation of CD107a expression and promotion of NK cell development. In addition, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 is significantly inhibited.

The presence of NK cells enhances the anti-fibrotic effect of the IL-10 gene in mice, leading to improved liver fibrosis and alleviation of liver damage. The IL-10 gene facilitates the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and boosts the upregulation of NKG2D, IFN-γ, and CD107a expressions, promoting NK cell development. Additionally, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and raises the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the beneficial effects of IL-10 on fibrosis are significantly hindered.

improve:In the presence of NK cells, IL-10 gene intervention significantly alleviated liver injury and collagen fiber deposition, improving mouse liver fibrosis. In addition, it promoted the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and enhanced their function, as evidenced by an increase in activation-related receptor NKG2D and toxic factors IFN-γ and CD107a. At the same time, it also regulated the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver accompanied by an increase in CCL5 content in the liver. However, when NK cells were exhausted from the liver, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly inhibited.

In the presence of NK cells, IL-10 gene intervention effectively reduced liver injury and collagen fiber deposition, thus improving liver fibrosis in mice. This intervention also promoted the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and enhanced their function, as observed by increased levels of the activation-related receptor NKG2D, and the production of IFN-γ and CD107a, which are toxic factors. Additionally, IL-10 gene intervention regulated the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver, accompanied by an elevation in CCL5 levels in the liver. However, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly impeded when NK cells were depleted from the liver.

Write a conclusion for these results:In the presence of NK cells, IL-10 gene intervention effectively reduced liver injury and collagen deposition, improving liver fibrosis in mice. This intervention enhanced the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and boosted their function, as evidenced by increased expression of activation-related receptor NKG2D, as well as the production of inflammatory factors IFN-γ and cytotoxic factor CD107a. Furthermore, IL-10 gene intervention regulated the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver tissues, accompanied by an elevation in CCL5 levels. However, when NK cells were depleted from the liver, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly inhibited.

In conclusion, our findings demonstrate that IL-10 gene intervention, in the presence of NK cells, is effective in reducing liver injury and collagen deposition, leading to an improvement in liver fibrosis in mice. This intervention not only increases the accumulation of NK cells in the liver but also enhances their functionality, as indicated by upregulated expression of NKG2D and increased production of IFN-γ and CD107a. Additionally, IL-10 gene intervention plays a role in regulating the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver tissues, potentially through the modulation of CCL5 levels. However, the depletion of NK cells from the liver significantly inhibits the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10. These findings highlight the potential of targeting NK cells and utilizing IL-10 gene intervention as a therapeutic approach for the management of liver fibrosis.
